title: PicoCTF Writeup тАФ DISKO2
markdown:
### ЁЯУЭ Challenge Overview

In this challenge, weтАЩre given a disk image file named `disko-2.dd.gz`. Our goal is to explore its contents and uncover the hidden flag. The hint is in the name: "DISKO" implies something to do with **partitions or disk-level forensics**. LetтАЩs dive in.

* * *

### ЁЯЫая╕П Step 1: Decompress the Disk Image

We start with a gzipped file. So first, we extract it using `gunzip`:

Copy

```
gunzip disko-2.dd.gz
```

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FSB0cNp74e9KLGByTLZv2%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520201913.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3D8813a9e0-5979-4b6d-9da5-1e3c97e7eb69&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=fa2688c1&sv=2)

This gives us the raw disk image: `disko-2.dd`.

* * *

### ЁЯФН Step 2: Inspect the Disk Partitions

Next, we inspect the disk structure using the `mmls` command from the Sleuth Kit:

Copy

```
mmls disko-2.dd
```

This outputs the partition layout:

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FCpOkNaUkjBoysPwwyoJU%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520201952.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3Dab07fc09-f3ed-44c3-9cad-aa6e4616d17c&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=325a5808&sv=2)

Copy

```
DOS Partition Table
...
002: 000:000   0000002048   0000053247   0000051200   Linux (0x83)
003: 000:001   0000053248   0000118783   0000065536   Win95 FAT32 (0x0b)
```

ЁЯФО So we have:

- A **Linux partition** starting at sector `2048`

- A **FAT32 partition** starting at `53248`

LetтАЩs begin with the Linux one.

* * *

### ЁЯУж Step 3: Extract the Linux Partition

We use `dd` to extract just the Linux partition from the disk image:

Copy

```
dd if=disko-2.dd of=Linux.img bs=512 skip=2048 count=51200
```

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FxWo76F0hXcCoknuCh9Qb%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520202021.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3Ddc6f5f07-a70b-4663-a462-9e43f4e59986&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=b66f3c3f&sv=2)

**Explanation**:

- `bs=512`: Block size (sectors)

- `skip=2048`: Start from sector 2048 (Linux partition)

- `count=51200`: Number of sectors (from mmls output)

This command gives us a clean file called `Linux.img` containing only the Linux partition.

* * *

### ЁЯФС Step 4: Search for the Flag

Now that we have the Linux partition as a standalone image, we can search through it.

A quick and effective method is to extract all readable strings and grep for the flag format:

Copy

```
strings Linux.img | grep "picoC"
```

![](https://omar-4.gitbook.io/omar-khalid/~gitbook/image?url=https%3A%2F%2F1803975356-files.gitbook.io%2F%7E%2Ffiles%2Fv0%2Fb%2Fgitbook-x-prod.appspot.com%2Fo%2Fspaces%252FoCigElGty18x5rSkroXb%252Fuploads%252FatMIlnmNdUimdN1NNymC%252FScreenshot%25202025-07-04%2520202058.png%3Falt%3Dmedia%26token%3De5696f61-b8cf-431f-85f9-69a824b1d85c&width=768&dpr=3&quality=100&sign=2c342079&sv=2)

And we find:

Copy

```
picoCTF{4_P4Rt_1t_i5_055dd175}
```

ЁЯОЙ **Flag Found!**

* * *

### ЁЯПБ Final Flag

Copy

```
picoCTF{4_P4Rt_1t_i5_055dd175}
```

* * *

### ЁЯТб Lessons Learned

- Always decompress `.gz` files before working with disk images.

- `mmls` is your friend when identifying disk partition layouts.

- `dd` can be used to surgically extract specific parts of a disk image.

- `strings` and `grep` make it easy to find flags in raw files.

- Don't forget to check all partitions тАФ this time, we got lucky with the first one!
